We haven't finalized our approach to drawing from conda-forge. However, we know it's *not* going to be a binary mirror; we will *always* rebuild. If we draw from a non-zero build number, it's likely that we will reset the build to zero. And of course, we may have technical or strategic reasons why we must modify the recipe or even patch the source slightly in defaults. 

Regardless, the point is that nobody should be depending on build numbers communicating any sort of comparative value across builds, and I oppose efforts to lead users to believe that they do.

> If "build number" should be truely a "build number" and not mean "recipe version", than it would be good to introduce another component to just mean that (or use `1.1` as `recipe_version.build_number`).

This is a very interesting idea. In fact, `VersionSpec` already supports the notion of a "local" version, specified with a '+'. Commonly this is used to append, say, the git commit hash, but it supports the same period-delimited structure that the standard version spec does. I could see channels adopting the local version as a recipe bump.

That version is compatible with your constraints. Features are not hard requirements---they instruct conda to install as many packages _as possible_ with the requested feature.

But it is indeed a long-standing problem that `conda` needs to provide better hints when arriving at a "suboptimal" solution, like a downgrade etc. It turns out that generating _informative_ hints is quite difficult.

There is never a need for an environment variable on Windows to contain double-quotes. Whereas on Unix, double quotes are used to prevent the delimiting of such env vars at spaces and do not make it into the content of the env. var, on Windows things are different and the env. var includes those quotes.

To see this:

```
set TEST="Hello World"
echo %TEST%
"Hello World"
```

Versus:

```
set TEST=Hello World
echo %TEST%
Hello World
```

Alternatively, you can do:

```
set "TEST=Hello World" if you want things to seem a bit more Unix-like, and in this case Windows will just discard them for you.

So this is a confusion that I've bumped into.  If you run activate.bat while in the root of your root environment, it doesn't drop you into a new folder.  You're still in the root of your root environment.  Guess what else is in that folder?  Python.exe - whatever version your root installation is.  Thus, it appears that you haven't activated the environment, but the real issue is that it's finding the local python.exe before it even goes looking for stuff on PATH.

CD into a different folder, and you should see the correct Python.

OK, I see what `lock_fs_path` is for. Forget the suggestion about the change of the path.

> That path is restored when you start the IDE, that's what slows down OCIDE. Unfortunately there is nothing I can do, the file system model is already filled in a background thread but threading in python is not always helpful due to the GIL.

Hm, I'm unsure about this, there must be a solution...

Quoting from https://wiki.python.org/moin/GlobalInterpreterLock

> The GIL is controversial because it prevents multithreaded CPython programs from taking full advantage of multiprocessor systems in certain situations. *Note that potentially blocking or long-running operations, such as I/O [...] happen outside the GIL*. Therefore it is only in multithreaded programs that spend a lot of time inside the GIL, interpreting CPython bytecode, that the GIL becomes a bottleneck.

I guess OCIDE could restore it when the file system view gets actually shown (=activated the first time), if it isn't used in the complete session then this issue will never arise. And it's quite clear what the problem is and the user has the option to directly change it afterwards.
What do you think about delaying the restore to this event?

@gsorrenti Yes VUnit supports compile order detection on component instantiation (There is none). You have to first understand that the problem to determine compile order dependency and the problem of determining a subset of files required to build a top level are slightly different problems. VUnit focuses only on the first. 

This difference is clear when it comes to component instantiation. There is no need to compile the entity intended to map to the component before compiling the architecture instantiating the component. When the entity intended to map to the component is changed there is no need to recompile the architecture instantiating it and vice versa. Thus there is **no** compile order dependency between the two. 

The only requirement is that both files have been compiled before starting elaboration which is when component binding occurs. VUnit will always compile all files before starting simulation and thus ensures this requirement is met. VUnit thus does not need to know the component binding to compile files in the correct order or to recompile the correct affected files. VUnit only needs to care if one file needs to be compiled before another file, never what subset of files are required to successfully elaborate a top level.

Thus I am saying that you are trying to use VUnit to solve a problem that is does not need to solve to perform its officially supported function. That is not to say that it is not possible to extend VUnit to support other use cases, a user implemented component dependency detection based on a simple heuristic in one of our internal classes: https://github.com/VUnit/vunit/pull/30. This is not something that we publicly support though. 

I can say though that we try to avoid creating the need to do a full VHDL parser with semantic analysis (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Semantic_analysis_(compilers)) since it is a lot of work. More work than the rest of VUnit combined. Precise component binding analysis requires semantic analysis such as the knowledge of all data types in the scope of the instantiation to select the match. People might theoretically define their own std_logic type in their own package, simple regex wont do the job.

To only make this setting visible for celery, you could set it as an environment variable for your Celery processes (`ELASTIC_APM_INSTRUMENT=false`).

It would be interesting to see if this issue also occurs with prefork workers if you have a way to test it without much work.

Also, with such a large amount of tasks per minute, we suggest to [sample](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/apm/agent/python/current/configuration.html#config-transaction-sample-rate) aggressively.

I'm not sure why `print` isn't honoring your locale or `PYTHONIOENCODING` in your case. The following is on Ubuntu 16.04:

```shell
$ python --version
Python 2.7.12
$ export LC_ALL=POSIX LC_CTYPE=POSIX LANG=POSIX
$ python -c 'print u"\xdc"'
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
UnicodeEncodeError: 'ascii' codec can't encode character u'\xdc' in position 0: ordinal not in range(128)
```

Setting `PYTHONIOENCODING` fixes the issue with no locale:
```shell
$ PYTHONIOENCODING=utf-8 python -c 'print u"\xdc"'
Ü
```

Setting the locale correctly and not specifying `PYTHONIOENCODING` prints correctly:
```shell
$ export LC_ALL=en_US.UTF-8 LC_CTYPE=en_US.UTF-8 LANG=en_US.UTF-8
$ python -c 'print u"\xdc"'
Ü
$ PYTHONIOENCODING=utf-8 python -c 'print u"\xdc"'
Ü
```

Since you are on 16.04, you could try installing `blobxfer` for `python3` instead which shouldn't have those `print` issues with unicode.

Hi Milan, 

In my testing I have found the following:

1. If my rpath = "/root/testpath/testdir" and is a directory, my lpath = "/testfolder/test", and the script being run is stored in C:/scriptlocation, then the directory being downloaded ends up in "C:/scriptlocation/root/testpath/testdir", so the rpath value is used instead of the lpath. This happens whether or not the lpath target directory actually exists or not. I would expect this would either result in the directory being saved to "C:/scriptlocation/testfolder/test/...." or if it was consistent with the single file download behaviour, to "C:/testfolder/test/....".

2. If my rpath = "/root/testpath/testdir" and is a directory, my lpath = "./testfolder/test" (note the dot at the front), and the script being run is stored in C:/scriptlocation, then the download fails whether or not the lpath target directory exists or not. If this was consistent with the single file download behaviour, it would download to "C:/scriptlocation/testfolder/test/...." given the use of the preceeding dot.

3. If my rpath = "/root/testpath/testdir/file.txt" and is a single file, my lpath = "/testfolder/test" , and the script being run is stored in C:/scriptlocation, then the download ends up being stored at "C:/testfolder/test", but is not actually file.txt, but a file with unknown extension called test. This is obviously a symptom of specifying a directory for the lpath and a single file for the rpath, but it is still concerning that the local path is no longer used relative to the script location, and the file is downloaded to "C:<lpath>" instead of "C:/scriptlocation/<lpath>", as I would expect that either the download fails or at least is still respecting the relative path from the script location rather than from C:. 

4. If my rpath = "/root/testpath/testdir/file.txt" and is a single file, my lpath = "./testfolder/test" (note the dot in front) , and the script being run is stored in C:/scriptlocation, then the download ends up being stored at "C:/scriptlocation/testfolder/test", but again is not actually file.txt, but a file with unknown extension called test. This is again obviously a symptom of specifying a directory for the lpath and a single file for the rpath, and in this case at least the lpath is respected relative to the script location, but this is different behaviour to when downloading a directory instead of a single file (ie. using a dot in front fails every time for directory downloads). 

5. If my rpath = "/root/testpath/testdir/file.txt" and is a single file, my lpath = "./testfolder/test/file.txt" (note the dot in front) , and the script being run is stored in C:/scriptlocation, then the download ends up being stored at "C:/scriptlocation/testfolder/test", and is actually file.txt as expected. This works if the target directory is already in existence or not.

6. If my rpath = "/root/testpath/testdir/file.txt" and is a single file, my lpath = "/testfolder/test/file.txt" (note the dot in front) , and the script being run is stored in C:/scriptlocation, then the download ends up being stored at "C:/testfolder/test", and is actually file.txt. This works if the target directory is already in existence or not, but is also inconsistent with the directory download version. 

So overall the downloading on a single file works relatively as expected, but the downloading of a directory does not. As it stands, as highlighted in 1, there is no way to download to the intended folder, whether relative to the script location or an absolute file path is provided.

This is the result of frequent and intensive IO due to very frequent progress state saving. The way progress is tracked needs to be tuned to result in less frequent reads and writes to disk, reducing the liklihood of thread locking and contention.

I'm closing this issue because I don't think we should take any action on it. I did do a little research and I think the problem is simply that's how OGR works; no streaming of GeoJSON input.

I'm guessing here but I think even if a machine has to start swapping it won't be too bad. If we find GeoJSON sources are causing RAM contention problems in production we can fix it then. Of the solutions I proposed above, I like the idea of using `ijson` best, doing our own GeoJSON parsing instead of using OGR. `sample.py` already has some code to read GeoJSON with ijson, it wouldn't be too hard to adapt it to convert the entire JSON input to a CSV file.

1.  As OA is expanding to be a repository of parcel polygon datasets as well as address points, sources that lack address fields should be allowed.  For this to work, the CI tests could allow sources that lack these fields as long as an `id` is present and maybe also if the data is of type `shapefile-polygon` or the ESRI layer is a polygon `geometryType`.  Machine could just ignore sources without address data when building the address point collections.
  
1. Related to the above, address point layers are frequently created from parcel polygons and retain the parcel identifier numbers.  Are we interested in capturing these in the `id` field?  If not, is it sufficient to just allow this data to be included because it can later be ignored?

However, one complication of inferring parcels from geometry would be that I believe there are some polygon sources that are actually building footprints and not parcels. I think the schema might need to be expanded to include a field indicating that the data are parcels.  The `id` field could potentially serve that purpose with proper documentation and the right testing in place.

> 12.04 is an LTS release just like 14.04 and 16.04 will be. Ubuntu supports LTS releases for at least 5 years, providing security and package updates. 12.04 is still a fine choice and is not EOL for another year and a half.

12.04 is a fine choice if you want "stable" (read: most bugs are known but probably won't be fixed) software. Package updates (but not upgrades) will be made available, but you will never be able to update an LTS to 1.0.2 that doesn't ship with it. 1.0.2 is absolutely a requirement for interacting with websites over TLS in 2015 and beyond. The state of the internet is that the 1024 bit roots are still *sort of* secure but each day that guarantee is being eroded further. Further, so many people are using outdated trust stores that they need to cross sign with the 1024 bit root and the 2048 bit roots. Beyond that, OpenSSL had no way of saying "Trust the shortest path" (which is the solution here) before 1.0.2{{some random letter that I think is "c"}}. So old OpenSSL versions need both roots otherwise they can't verify the whole chain of signatures. Note: this also happens if your chain that the server sends is incomplete. Browsers will work just fine because most browsers have logic to fetch the missing intermediate parts but plain, vanilla, OpenSSL doesn't. Even new OpenSSL doesn't provide this, but the fact is, OpenSSL needs to be updated to function properly in this day and age.

> Regarding opt-in, I am not sure what time interval makes sense. I honestly don't know enough about the subject matter to say what is reasonable, since I am not a security researcher. That's why I use certifi: because I can't put together an equivalent thing on my own (and I appreciate the work that gets put into it).

No time interval makes sense. That's the problem. And you parenthetical seems at odds with your next statement. "I appreciate you doing this for free, but let me be adversarial for no particularly good reason."

> However, when a breaking change is released that brings production sites to a halt it is difficult justifying trusting it.

Perhaps you shouldn't be haphazardly deploying to production? Continuous deployment is great, provided you test what's going to production before it reaches production.

> You mention that explicit failures get fixed faster. In this case that won't happen. Since the next LTS Ubuntu release is not due for another 7 months, the solution we went with was simply to pin certifi to the pervious version. It will likely stay this way until we are able to get openssl 1.0.2, some time in 2016. I suspect most people are doing this right now, or are using certifi.old_where(), both of which effectively negate the increased security the new version provides. Pinning the certifi version also will prevent any future updates going out which will mean that lots of codebases will become less secure, not more.

So you're implying that you pin and forget? You never revise your dependencies or versions there of? That sounds like a process failure on your end, not a problem with making certifi as a whole default to a more secure set of certificates.

> It just feels like there should be a better solution than dropping a huge breaking change over the weekend. Distros and browsers manage to stay on top managing root certs without suddenly breaking sites like amazon.com, paypal.com, ups.com, etc.

Actually you're woefully wrong. Distros did break this and then rushed out a fix for it when they realized that it did break things. There are Fedora, Debian, and Ubuntu bugs about this (and those are just the ones I remember reading). Fedora has a way to disable these legacy certificates and I believe is planning to do so soon (just as soon as openssl 1.0.2 is their default openssl). None of them are happy about having to do this.

As I mentioned, browsers tend to use totally different mechanisms and allow things that should be broken and can cause security issues (fetching intermediate roots when they're missing for example).

> Is there some type of process that they follow that allows them to provide such safety that certifi could mirror?

Mozilla uses libnss that has been unaffected by this problem for a long time. I see no way for certifi to benefit from that. The problem is fundamentally that the internet is an awful place. There is no good time to break things because if you never break something, it never gets updated. If people didn't start disabling SSLv2 and RC4 ciphers (most certainly breaking some people using ancient versions of Windows) security would still be hindered by that lagging tail of users. Software that has a security impact has to charge forward for the benefit of all users at the sacrifice of breaking some.

Let me start out by saying that I don't have any experience using AMD and RequireJS and I have a lot of experience using CommonJS and Webpack. Webpack is great because it allows you to use a lot of the good things about Node.js (CommonJS, npm) within the browser by bundling all of your modules into a file that can be used within a `<script>` tag. With Webpack, it's easy to use external modules (such as browser libraries like jQuery and Bootstrap) from within the bundle without actually bundling them using the `externals` config. This would allow the core notebook (bundled by Webpack) to access extensions (not bundled). However, I don't know of any good/safe way to allow extensions to access the notebook core, but like @blink1073 mentioned, there are a couple Webpack plugins that would enable this:

* [DllPlugin and DllReferencePlugin](https://github.com/webpack/docs/wiki/list-of-plugins#dllplugin): Webpack plugin that allows one bundle to provide modules (e.g. vendor bundle) to another consumer bundle (e.g. app bundle). I couldn't find an example of consuming a DLL bundle outside of Webpack (only examples of a provider bundle using the DllPlugin and a consumer using the DllReferencePlugin).
* [script-loader](https://webpack.github.io/docs/shimming-modules.html#exposing): Webpack loader that will evaluate a module in the global context (like a `<script>` tag).
* [expose-loader](https://webpack.github.io/docs/shimming-modules.html#exposing): Webpack loader that will export a module to the global context.

It appears that all of these options would enable extensions to access the notebook core by exposing the notebook core to the global context, which is not a good solution.

> I expect that we should be able to deal with this in JupyterLab by making the extension points more clearly defined exported functions / events / observables / etc., Rather than allowing full access to everything in extensions. If we do that, extensions shouldn't need to be able to extend prototypes on Cell, etc. which is the source of the problems we have in the notebook right now (and then the fact that they cannot extend these prototypes becomes a good thing).

I have experience developing Atom packages and if you look at Atom's API, it also exposes an [`atom` global](https://atom.io/docs/api/v1.9.8/AtomEnvironment#instance-confirm) that allows packages to subscribe to events, get state, and safely set state. Instead of "extending the prototype of a [TextEditor]," a package can render custom UI within a TextEditor by [calling a method](https://github.com/nteract/hydrogen/blob/ac31c110903ae2d6009624441521116dbb840aac/lib/main.coffee#L243) on that editor. 

I'm not familiar with the current notebook- or jupyterlab-extension API, so I would need to see some docs/examples to offer any suggestions regarding *to Webpack or not to Webpack*.

@rgbkrk I know that nteract is using Electron (the same native shell that Atom uses) and therefore doesn't require Webpack, but do you have any insights nonetheless?

> Also, parsing errors (inherent to the input) and validity errors (logical constraints that may change) are fundamentally different, so IMO it's a distinction worth preserving.

Agree, but this needs some clarification on what should be treated as parsing error and what should be validity errors. E.g. currently incorrect date format is treated as parsing error. I'm not sure that's correct approach. Let me explain. Obviously there are 2 steps: import and validation.

1\. Import. IMO the only thing that should be validated during this step is 'shape' of data. I.e. detect rogue fields and check required fields (if `partial=False`) then follow nested fields and check the same for nested objects. As far as data does not satisfy shape constraints - it doesn't make sense to validate it - because model level validation often requires several fields to be present in expected positions. This is the case where separation between parsing and validation errors shines.

2\. Validation. Here any other constraints should be checked - type errors, format errors, 'greater than zero' constraints like. And ideally there should be 2 modes - 'fail early' - give first occurred validation error and 'show all' - give list with all errors.

I'm not saying it's the only right way, but that's how I see it.

Given model from your comment:

```
m = M({'datefield':'impossible_value', 'intfield': 5})
```

should pass

```
m.validate()
```

should give 

```
schematics.exceptions.ModelValidationError: {
  'datefield': [u'Could not parse impossible_value. Should be ISO8601.'], 
  'intfield': [u'Int value should be greater than 9.']
}
```

```
m = M({'intfield': 5})
```
should fail with

```
schematics.exceptions.ModelValidationError: {
  'datefield': [u'This field is required.'],
}
```

```
m = M({'blah': 5})
```
should fail with 

```
schematics.exceptions.ModelValidationError: {
  'datefield': [u'This field is required.'],
  'blah': [u'Rogue field']
}
```

Unfortunately it doesn't work like that now. In case of DateType - parse error occurs even if you're sending date in incorrect format or breaking some format constraints although shape of the object is correct, datefield is of the string type and only breaks month limit.

```
>>> M({'intfield': '1', 'datefield': '2015-13-12'})
ModelConversionError: {'datefield': ['Could not parse 2015-13-12. Should be ISO8601 (YYYY-MM-DD).']}
```

The TRANSPORT error represent connection errors with the broker, typically connection closed or connection refused.
The error_cb should give you more context through the error string.

If you are on osx this is typically caused by 'localhost' resolving to both ipv4 and ipv6 but the broker is only listening on one of those address families, in which case you can set 'broker.address.family': 'v4'.

But yes, you are correct, TRANSPORT errors can be ignored, they are typically transient.

```
HAS_FAST = False
try:
    from fastavro.reader import read_data

    HAS_FAST = True
except:
    pass
```

So it checks if `fastavro` is available (and it always is, because it is in `pip`'s dependencies) it uses it everywhere after because `HAS_AVRO` is `True` now.

And `fastavro` don't seem to be able to handle all the avro cases. And when it can't you have a problem: there is no "runtime" per-message fallback to "normal" avro. It will always try using this "optimisation".

> why would fastavro be available at all

@edenhill Python's built-in logging is considered best practice, so it's not crazy to go directly to that. However, obviously the callback gives more flexibility. Not everyone uses the built-in (e.g. afaik some WSGI servers like uWSGI have their own logging mechanism). But those might end up just being different handlers in Python's logging (or possible to be adapted to as such).

In terms of multiple threads, I'm not worried about coalescing them all in C and then processing in Python as long as messages within a thread can be maintained in order. However, option 1 does have the drawback that logs can potentially get backlogged. This may always be a problem in python due to the GIL, so I assume there is some back pressure. If there is, then using a shared C queue only temporarily alleviates the issue. Putting back pressure on the original threads might actually be a better solution. (And if there isn't back pressure, then there is an infinite queue that needs fixing.)

Option 2 seems bad if logs can have long delays before processing. This seems like it will lead to debugging nightmares since logs that should have been written may not have been. Any async processing can result in this case, but at least the background thread should be processing the messages as fast as possible.

Option 1 and piping directly to Python's logging library seems the easiest for the user, but has the lag problem. Option 1 and leaving it up to the user to implement the callback provides flexibility. Processing log messages directly and eating the cost of GIL and python code to process the message (which we may want to better understand the relative costs of) seems the "most correct" solution in that you make the threads deal with the general slowness of the rest of the code. I'm curious to know what the actual cost of the last option is, or what the exact implications are for that thread of things move slow (e.g. what if you were just on a slow processor? would that cause things to break?).

Hm. This requires more thought, but I think this may be the correct behavior, since there is no 31st of June:

```python
>>> from dateutil.relativedelta import relativedelta
>>> from datetime import datetime
>>> d0 = datetime(1991, 1, 30)
>>> d1 = datetime(1991, 1, 31)
>>> d2 = datetime(1991, 6, 30)
>>> d0 + relativedelta(months=5)
datetime.datetime(1991, 6, 30, 0, 0)
>>> d1 + relativedelta(months=5)
datetime.datetime(1991, 6, 30, 0, 0)
>>> d2 - relativedelta(months=5)
datetime.datetime(1991, 1, 30, 0, 0)
```

It's not clear that there is a "right" answer here, since it is a sort of ill-defined question, and in cases where there are two possible right choices, I think keeping the old behavior is better.

However, it might be worth considering adding an interface giving the other behavior.

I did some triaging and the following seems to be the case:
The compiler used an invalid jumpdest errors as a means to halt the execution. However, laser assumes that jumps always go to a correct jumpdest, so when it encounters the jumpi it will determine that 0x0002 is not a valid jump destination and ignore that jump fully (also the negated branch).

**solution**
To fix the problem the check on a valid jumpdestination should be moved, so that the branch for the negated condition of the jumpi is still executed.

Thanks everyone for the thoughtful responses!

After looking a bit further, I would say that NetCDF has the most potential and the best "alignment" to this particular dataset that I have in mind. (pandas' Dataframe still seems less well aligned to me, but it's probably doable.) With netCDF, the data formatting and storage would be more standardized than what I suggested, and I agree that units are best kept in the metadata and out of the data's indexes. It seems that the standardization part with netCDF involves coming up with "conventions", analogous to the [CF conventions](http://cfconventions.org/) and its [units](https://pypi.org/project/cfunits/), that cover the various PV performance measurement/monitoring cases. (Perhaps one aligns the conventions with the eventual Orange Button taxonomy?) The devil might be in the details, though, so I am going to report back after I try to actually implement netCDF for my particular data at hand.

Regarding some of @cwhanse 's more specific comments:

- I would have to play around with the tuple indexing. I'm hoping that netCDF will allow better "simultaneous slicing" of all four components from a single I-V-F-T curve.

- The values used in the keys are the "nominal" F and T values for identifying a particular curve. The actual values (that may vary) during the I-V curve measurement are in the data vectors. This particular case is of "uncorrected" I-V curves taken in a measurement lab with a solar simulator that "flickers" and possible heating of the device during the I-V measurement. I suppose outdoor data would usually just have a single F and T for each curve, or possibly two values from pre- and post-curve sweep. I suppose that ideally one might like to have accurate timestamps on all the points, which I think would allow one to cover all these different setups more generally in the data structure. The calibration labs I know of don't record such timestamps :(. Indeed they usually don't actually record separate temperatures at each point in the sweep, which may be justified for fast sweeps where the 3 sig-fig temperature gauge doesn't respond quickly enough.

- I agree that a wrapper class looks like an eventuality. However, we should strive to make the data structure as easy to query as possible "in the raw", which I see as a particular strength of pandas.

- I think I switched to F because NREL used E for spectral irradiance, and, more importantly, my particular "effective irradiance" is defined as a ratio of short-circuit currents, F = Isc/Isc0, where Isc is at prevailing POA spectral+total irradiance and junction temperature and Isc0 is at reference conditions. This is also the value reported by a reference monitoring device via F = Isc/Isc0 = M*Isc,r/Isc0,r, where the spectral correction factor M changes with the temperature of both devices and there is a linearity assumption w.r.t. current vs. total irradiance (and, of course, a homogeneity assumption). This differs a bit from some of the [other "flavors" of effective irradiance](https://pvpmc.sandia.gov/modeling-steps/2-dc-module-iv/effective-irradiance/).

The Matlab toolbox uses location structs in a few places, e.g.:

https://github.com/Sandia-Labs/PVLIB_Matlab/blob/master/PVLIB/pvl_makelocationstruct.m

https://github.com/Sandia-Labs/PVLIB_Matlab/blob/master/PVLIB/pvl_clearsky_ineichen.m

https://github.com/Sandia-Labs/PVLIB_Matlab/blob/master/PVLIB/pvl_ephemeris.m

Rob's original Python port copied most of this behavior:

https://github.com/Sandia-Labs/PVLIB_Python/blob/master/pvlib/pvl_makelocationstruct.py

https://github.com/Sandia-Labs/PVLIB_Python/blob/master/pvlib/pvl_clearsky_ineichen.py

https://github.com/Sandia-Labs/PVLIB_Python/blob/master/pvlib/pvl_ephemeris.py

One place where the Matlab code and the original Python code diverged was in the TMY reader results, also known as "meta". The Matlab [version](https://github.com/Sandia-Labs/PVLIB_Matlab/blob/master/PVLIB/pvl_readtmy3.m) put data and metadata into the same struct, where as the Python [version](https://github.com/Sandia-Labs/PVLIB_Python/blob/e74644ba99d16113fb976493b5006ffdf7fb1671/pvlib/pvl_readtmy3.py) had to break out the metadata into a dict so that the data could be in a DataFrame. 

I wanted PVLIB_Python to use the patterns and terminology of a standard Python library, so I created a PR (Sandia-Labs/PVLIB_Python#17) that replaced the location struct with a ``Location`` class.

In retrospect, I believe that was a missed opportunity to drop the ``location`` object/struct arguments in favor of latitude, longitude, tz arguments.

I think my completely-over-engineered-track-progress-timer is ready for review with the push of https://github.com/jcass77/Mopidy-MusicBox-Webclient/commit/67706a2202a4d1c77dc4c7725a86fc45a746f5d7.

Changes:

- now includes a few wrapper functions for media-progress-timer to implement the 'reference timer / heartbeat' idea. I'm sure these could be integrated in a more useful form if there is sufficient interest for people to re-use.

- the progress timer now accounts for any buffering that Mopidy may be doing in order to give the **actual** time position, which should hopefully be within 500ms of the audio.

- it includes an exponential step-back so that the sync is checked less and less often as the track progresses and the progress timer remains in sync. The default is: 500ms, 1, 2, 4, and 8 seconds and then every 30 seconds thereafter while the track is playing. This is just a workaround while the Mopidy events described in https://github.com/mopidy/mopidy/issues/1420 are not available.

- I used the time elapsed indicator on the left of the slider to output some debug info while doing the development, and in the end I thought these were useful enough (and kinda cool looking) to consider keeping in the final product:

  - ``(wait)`` - progress timer is waiting for Mopidy to provide the target position.

  - ``(sync)`` - timer is busy syncing (i.e. Mopidy is buffering).

  - ``m:ss`` - timer is in sync and elapsed time is displayed normally.

There are a currently a few deficiencies that I am not happy with.

* The `Z3Config.cmake` file does not state where the `z3` binary is. This is slightly complicated due to the fact that in a multi configuration generator (e.g. Visual Studio) there may be multiple builds of `z3`. One idea I have for handling this to export the `shell` target (this is Z3's internal name for the `z3` executable) as `z3::shell` and then require the client of `Z3Config.cmake` to inspect the exported target's properties to determine the location of the binary. I haven't done this so far because I really didn't want to leak the `shell` target to the outside world unless I have to.

* The example projects (and any other project) will fail to build if `libz3` is built as a static library and we use OpenMP. This is because we don't export the requirement that `libz3.a` needs OpenMP. I need to have a think about how to fix this. In the past I found the problem was that doing `target_link_libraries(libz3 INTERFACE ${OPENMP_C_FLAGS})` broke the MSVC build because it interpreted `/openmp` as a file to link against... I need to do some testing on Windows and see if I can come up with something that works.

* I've not created a `Z3Version.cmake` file. This file would be used if the caller of `find_package(Z3 CONFIG)` specified a version. I'm not sure if it's worth going to the effort of doing this.

> splitting up the layer docs into separate files should be quick

Unfortunately, we cannot easily split it up because of the following Sphinx bug: https://github.com/sphinx-doc/sphinx/issues/1822
In short, this bug prevents the following to work:
```
Helper functions
----------------

.. autosummary::
    :nosignatures:
    
    get_output
    get_output_shape
```

> as you need to know exactly which variant of the ResNet block the authors are using

True, the paper is not clear about whether they use the variant of the first or second ResNet paper. But at least the code is online at https://github.com/JianGoForIt/YellowFin.

> I have no idea what that means

This part is clear from Table 2 in Appendix E: they use pointwise (1x1) convolutions in the residual blocks. See Figure 5 in the [original ResNet paper](http://arxiv.org/pdf/1512.03385.pdf) if you're interested for more.

But I'd just use CIFAR-10, not CIFAR-100, to keep it simple.

> I personally would opt out for just using the existing Lasagne Recipe for 50 layer ResNet and plug it there.

The ResNet-50 Recipe just shows how to use the ResNet-50 from Caffe's model zoo, it does not have training included. But there's another one for ResNet-56 that includes training on CIFAR-10: https://github.com/Lasagne/Recipes/blob/master/papers/deep_residual_learning/Deep_Residual_Learning_CIFAR-10.py
In addition, there's the DenseNet recipe which also includes training for CIFAR-10: https://github.com/Lasagne/Recipes/tree/master/papers/densenet
And a recipe for the second ResNet paper which includes ResNet-110 and ResNet-164 with preactivation: https://github.com/Lasagne/Recipes/tree/master/papers/preactivation_and_wide_resnet

This could be a nice addition indeed. I also think `shared_axes` would be overkill -- that use case is better served by using `ReshapeLayer`s and `DimshuffleLayer`s imo, or even custom `Layer` subclasses.

I think "the number of axes not to be flattened" is the wrong way to think about it, because everything gets flattened into a matrix, we're just influencing which of the original axes form part of the rows of this matrix, and which form part of the columns. This is very different from e.g. `FlattenLayer`'s `outdim` argument, which directly controls how many trailing axes get flattened (and the number of dimensions in the output depends on this, whereas in this case it would always be 2). So to avoid confusion we should probably choose the language carefully.

As for the name, I think I prefer something with `distribute`, or maybe something else altogether, but `keep_axes` is not clear enough.

I've never noticed this issue before (which is really because I never paid any attention to `num_find` while using Veritesting). My guess is, that with Veritesting enabled, it does respect the `num_find` setting during its core static symbolic execution procedure (which is implemented in `angr/analyses/veritesting.py`). When it decides to exit from static execution phase, `PathGroup` checks if there are more than `num_find` paths found, and if there are, it quits from the iteration and returns all the result. And it's totally possible to get more than one `found` paths during static symbolic execution.

If my guess is right, the solution would be passing `num_find` into Veritesting.

On Wed, 25 May 2016, Alex Black wrote:

> I'm trying to figure out how to fly the copter with RC channel overrides, it never leaves the ground, any ideas what I'm doing wrong? Thanks!

Here's your example modified so that It Works For Me.  The ctitical 
problem was that you are doing something with "channel_override" rather 
han "channel.overrides"...

Standard disclaimers apply: using RC channels like this to move your 
aircraft around is probably a very bad idea....



#!/usr/bin/python

import dronekit
import dronekit_sitl
import time


lat = 49.2778638
lon = -123.0633418

sitl = dronekit_sitl.SITL()
sitl.download('copter', '3.3', verbose=True)
sitl_args = ['-I0', '--model', 'quad', 
'--home={0},{1},584,353'.format(lat, lon)]
sitl.launch(sitl_args, await_ready=True, restart=True)
connection_string = 'tcp:127.0.0.1:5760'


vehicle = dronekit.connect(connection_string, wait_ready=True)

# Don't let the user try to arm until autopilot is ready
while not vehicle.is_armable:
   print(" Waiting for vehicle to initialise... (GPS={0}, 
Battery={1})".format(vehicle.gps_0, vehicle.battery))
   time.sleep(1)

# Set vehicle mode
desired_mode = 'STABILIZE'
while vehicle.mode != desired_mode:
   vehicle.mode = dronekit.VehicleMode(desired_mode)
   time.sleep(0.5)

while not vehicle.armed:
     print("Arming motors")
     vehicle.armed = True
     time.sleep(0.5)

while True:
   vehicle.channels.overrides[3] = 2000
   print " Ch3 override: %s" % vehicle.channels.overrides[3]

   if vehicle.location.global_relative_frame.alt >= 140:
     print('Reached target altitude: 
{0:.2f}m'.format(vehicle.location.global_relative_frame.alt))
     break
   else:
     print("Altitude: 
{0:.2f}m".format(vehicle.location.global_relative_frame.alt))
   time.sleep(0.5)

@wilco5nine The problem is probably that you have multiple installations of Python on your machine. The version on CLI is not associated with the PyPi where you installed dronekit-python (which explains that error). The version accessed via IDLE is associated with this PyPi but it is the older version of Dk-Python present.

Fixing the issue with multiple Python installations is something I'd explore via Google :-0. It's not a DK-Python issue really. Last time something like this happened I removed all Python installations and reinstalled.

The error then is because we haven't uploaded a new version of DK-Python to PyPi - this something @peterbarker hopes to do soon. In the meantime, you can reinstall dk-python from github like this:
```
sudo pip install git+https://github.com/dronekit/dronekit-python -UI
```
at that point, your IDLE version will probably work.

This was discussed before, in #93 and #103, and the main issue was that the coefficient lengths for some padding modes aren't equal, so coefficients for >1D transform cannot be tightly packed into an array. This mismatch in lengths is also used to encode the fact that the input array was odd-length, so that information would be permanently lost.

```
>>> coefs = pywt.wavedec(np.ones(10), 'haar', 'periodization')
>>> list(map(len, coefs))
[2, 2, 3, 5] # 2 + 2 != 3
>>> rec = pywt.waverec(coefs, 'haar')
>>> len(rec)
10 # Note odd length step at 1st level is preserved
```

pyrealsense2 is a direct port of the C++ API.
In the example you linked, the helper class `device_container` is defined to help manage the devices.
On line 169 this class is used to start the devices with the `device_container::enable_device()` function, which is implemented on line 30.
Inside `device_container::enable_device()`'s implementation, you can see on lines 45-50 that a separate pipeline/config object is created for each one. This code can be ported directly to python.
line 52 holds the relevant objects alive inside the `device_container` object. Note that only the profile and pipeline are stored, not the config object.
`device_container::poll_frames()`, implemented on line 97, is how the example accesses the frames from the devices, simply polling each in turn (lines 101-114). This code can also be ported directly.

Hope this helps!

The answer is somewhat different for D400 series and the SR300.
For the SR300, you need a Kernel version that supports the device. As a work-around you could build the library with `-DFORCE_LIBUVC=true` to handle everything in user-space, but generally speaking you want a proper kernel driver. 
The way to get this is either upgrade to a Kernel version that supports the device (4.12 and up), or deal with manual Kernel patching. We offer patches specialized for 4.4, 4.8 and 4.10 kernels, and scripts that we try to keep up to date. This is an uphill battle since every distribution is switching Kernel versions and adding custom patches all the time. 
In addition we offer `realsense-uvcvideo` DKMS package that we test regularly with out-of-the-box Ubuntu 16 and fix when necessary. Any changes you make to the machine (like installing additional Kernel version) are likely to require additional troubleshooting. We are happy to assist in such cases, to the extent that we can. 
The D400 series will be recognized out of the box by any modern Kernel (since 4.4.0.59) and only the advanced functionality ([UVC metadata](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/blob/master/doc/frame_metadata.md#metadata-support-for-intel-realsense-devices)) will require a Kernel patch. Like with the SR300, full device support will eventually be a part of the Linux kernel, but these things take time. 

You are making valid points about redistribution and the complexity of building from source, but we are doing what we can to improve the situation. During 2017, we have upstreamed SR300 support, provided pre-compiled DKMS, significantly expanded [installation.md](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/blob/master/doc/installation.md) and added [troubleshooting WIKI](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/wiki/Troubleshooting-Q&A).

Hello @bellekci , the SDK is intended to convey the depth data that is generated on device.
And while your request is technically feasible, it is out of the scope of the SDK.

On an implementation side:
Most stereo-based algo require that the input (left+right)images are aligned/rectified.
The Full HD resolution for infrared streams is a calibration format that provides unrectified images - intrinsic parameters are not calculated. 

You may also find useful the following paper that provides an in-depth review of the technology and the algorithms: https://arxiv.org/pdf/1705.05548.pdf

```python
# All searches should be executed as both POST and GET requests. Search outputs
# should match. The tear down process for all tests is to destroy created
# users. This process is not set in stone. See:
# https://github.com/PulpQE/pulp-smash/issues/5
#
# Dependency tree::
#
# SearchTestCase
# |-- SearchDetailsTestCase
# |-- SearchSortTestCase
# |-- SearchFieldTestCase
# |-- SearchFieldsTestCase
# `-- SearchIdTestCase
#     `-- SearchIdsTestCase
#         |-- SearchSkipTestCase
#         `-- SearchLimitTestCase

# SearchTestCase: Ask for users.
# Set up: Create one user.
{'criteria': {}}
{'criteria': {'filters': {}}}

# SearchDetailsTestCase: Ask that search results contain details.
# Set up: Create one user.
{'criteria': {}, 'details': true}

# SearchSortTestCase: Ask that search results be sorted.
# Set up: Create two users.
{'criteria': {}, 'sort': [['id', 'descending']]}

# SearchFieldTestCase: Ask that search results only contain a specified field.
# Set up: Create one user.
{'criteria': {}, 'field': 'id'}

# SearchFieldsTestCase: Ask that search results only contain specified fields.
# Set up: Create one user with login, roles and name attributes.
{'criteria': {}, 'fields': ['login', 'roles']}

# SearchIdTestCase: Ask for a user with a specific ID.
# Set up: Create two users.
{'criteria': {'filters': {'id': '…'}}}

# SearchIdsTestCase: Ask for users with one of several IDs.
# Set up: Create three users.
{'criteria': {'filters': {'id': {'$in': [id1, id2]}}}}

# SearchSkipTestCase: Ask for search results to be skipped.
# Set up: Create two users.
{'criteria': {'filters': {'id': {'$in': [id1, id2]}}}, 'skip': 1}

# SearchLimitTestCase: Ask for a limited number of results to be returned.
# Set up: Create two users.
{'criteria': {'filters': {'id': {'$in': [id1, id2]}}}, 'limit': 1}
```

Not quite.  The idea is to view the IPv6 Hop Limit value in the packet.  Can you try the `-vv` option?  If that doesn’t fully decode the IPv6 header, then capturing the pcap file would allow using wireshark to dissect the packets.

On an related topic, I think I need to revisit how the FCS is handled again. Currently, Spinel removes the FCS from the packet if it isn't provided, and indicates this with a flag in the metadata. If the radio actually supports FCS, then the FCS will only be present for inbound frames, not outbound frames. This means that outbound frames are actually two bytes shorter than inbound frames in that case.

`wpantund` handles this (and other things, like RSSI, etc) by using the [PPI](http://www.cacetech.com/documents/PPI%20Header%20format%201.0.7.pdf) DLT, which allows you to both specify the DLT per packet as well as append additional metadata like RSSI, etc.

I think I'd rather move to an approach where the method outlined here "just works", without needing to introspect metadata. Sure, the CRCs will read as zero (and, thus, as an error) when they aren't set, but lots of sniffer implementations do that for outbound packets---and it's better than (effectively) corrupting the packet by interpreting the last two non-FCS bytes of the frame as an actual FCS.

The above proposed change wouldn't affect this commit other than making it behave consistently.
Thoughts?

There is a fundamental difference in the testing between Windows and Linux here. The Linux code is running the OpenThread library as an executable binary that virtualizes the sends between each instance with a socket. The Windows code is testing the OpenThread library running in the driver, with the sends being virtualized in a virtual miniport driver (not included here yet). To call into the driver and instantiate new virtual miniport instances, the `otNodeApi` binary was added, and provide the simplest interface possible for the Python code to call. The exact same code being used for Windows could be used as a foundation for testing OpenThread via wpantund on Linux, if support was added for virtual devices.

I'm not sure what you mean by "smart bridge" mode, that is not a traditional networking term.  [Bridging](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bridging_(networking)) is not generally recommended given the resources disparities between Thread and other link technologies like WiFi and Ethernet.  Instead, [routers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Router_(computing)) should be used to connect Thread networks to other IPv6 networks.

In any case, OpenThread provides an API to send/receive raw IPv6 datagrams, which may be used for routing IPv6 datagrams to/from other IPv6 networks.  The [NCP](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/tree/master/src/ncp) implementation uses this capability to transfer IPv6 datagrams to/from [wpantund](https://github.com/openthread/wpantund/).

```
ThreadError otSendIp6Datagram(otInstance *aInstance, otMessage aMessage);

void otSetReceiveIp6DatagramCallback(otInstance *aInstance, otReceiveIp6DatagramCallback aCallback,
                                     void *aCallbackContext);
```

Hi, if you use the same images that you have used for training you should see good results as indeed your training accuracy is very high. So if you get a wrong classification result for the same images as you say, it almost certainly has to do with your preprocessing of the images and not with the network topology.

My guess would be that you do not normalize your images the same way in the test as you do in the training. But i cant tell for sure since you didnt show the code you used for the network training. 

To be more specific if you load an image with 
`img = scipy.ndimage.imread(args.image, mode="RGB")` 
it will have values from [0, 255]. 

After your transform 
`img = scipy.misc.imresize(img, (227, 227), interp="bicubic").astype(np.float32, casting='unsafe')` 
it will still have values in the range [0.0, 255.0] but in your training your images will probably have been normalized to the range [0.0, 1.0]. So in that case it would be perfectly explainable that you test and training performance is that much different.

Hi @fffupeng!
I use an additional script to do that. 

```"""
Tensorflow graph freezer
Converts Tensorflow trained models in .pb

Code adapted from:
https://gist.github.com/morgangiraud/249505f540a5e53a48b0c1a869d370bf#file-medium-tffreeze-1-py
"""

import os, argparse
os.environ['TF_CPP_MIN_LOG_LEVEL'] = '3'
import tensorflow as tf
from tensorflow.python.framework import graph_util

def freeze_graph(model_folder,output_graph="frozen_model.pb"):
    # We retrieve our checkpoint fullpath
    try:
        checkpoint = tf.train.get_checkpoint_state(model_folder)
        input_checkpoint = checkpoint.model_checkpoint_path
        print("[INFO] input_checkpoint:", input_checkpoint)
    except:
        input_checkpoint = model_folder
        print("[INFO] Model folder", model_folder)

    # Before exporting our graph, we need to precise what is our output node
    # This is how TF decides what part of the Graph he has to keep and what part it can dump
    output_node_names = "FullyConnected/Softmax" # NOTE: Change here

    # We clear devices to allow TensorFlow to control on which device it will load operations
    clear_devices = True
    
    # We import the meta graph and retrieve a Saver
    saver = tf.train.import_meta_graph(input_checkpoint + '.meta', clear_devices=clear_devices)

    # We retrieve the protobuf graph definition
    graph = tf.get_default_graph()
    input_graph_def = graph.as_graph_def()

    # We start a session and restore the graph weights
    with tf.Session() as sess:
        saver.restore(sess, input_checkpoint)

        # We use a built-in TF helper to export variables to constants
        output_graph_def = graph_util.convert_variables_to_constants(
            sess,                        # The session is used to retrieve the weights
            input_graph_def,             # The graph_def is used to retrieve the nodes 
            output_node_names.split(",") # The output node names are used to select the usefull nodes
        ) 

        # Finally we serialize and dump the output graph to the filesystem
        with tf.gfile.GFile(output_graph, "wb") as f:
            f.write(output_graph_def.SerializeToString())
        print("%d ops in the final graph." % len(output_graph_def.node))

        print("[INFO] output_graph:",output_graph)
        print("[INFO] all done")


if __name__ == '__main__':
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description="Tensorflow graph freezer\nConverts trained models to .pb file",
                                     prefix_chars='-')
    parser.add_argument("--mfolder", type=str, help="model folder to export")
    parser.add_argument("--ograph", type=str, help="output graph name", default="frozen_model.pb")
    
    args = parser.parse_args()
    print(args,"\n")

    freeze_graph(args.mfolder,args.ograph)
```

    import numpy as np
    from matplotlib import pyplot as plt
    from pyfftw.builders import fftn as build_fftn
    from pyfftw.builders._utils import next_fast_len

    all_l = np.arange(2, 1000)
    all_nl = np.zeros_like(all_l)
    all_times = np.zeros(len(all_l))
    all_times_pad = np.zeros(len(all_l))
    time_ratios = np.ones(len(all_l))
    for cnt, l in enumerate(all_l):
        l = int(l)
        print("l = {}".format(l))
        nl = next_fast_len(l)
        all_nl[cnt] = nl
        dl = np.random.randn(l)
        dnl = np.random.randn(nl)

        fft_l = build_fftn(dl, threads=1)
        fft_nl = build_fftn(dnl, threads=1)
        if l < 100:
            nreps = 10000
        elif l < 1000:
            nreps = 1000
        else:
            nreps = 100
        tstart = time.time()
        for n in range(nreps):
            fft_l(dl)
        tl = (time.time() - tstart) / nreps

        if l == nl:
            all_times[cnt] = tl
            all_times_pad[cnt] = tl
            time_ratios[cnt] == 1
            continue

        tstart = time.time()
        for n in range(nreps):
            fft_nl(dnl)
        tnl = (time.time() - tstart) / nreps

        all_times[cnt] = tl
        all_times_pad[cnt] = tnl
        time_ratios[cnt] = all_times_pad[cnt] / all_times[cnt]

    plt.figure(); plt.plot(all_l, 1e3*all_times, all_l, 1e3*all_times_pad)
    plt.legend(['unpadded', 'pad to next fast len'], loc='upper left')
    plt.xlabel('unpadded FFT length')
    plt.ylabel('time (ms)')
```

The most recent two commits are an unpolished implementation of having `pybind11_add_module` being accessible from installed pybind. Essentially the main CMakeLists.txt contains only code relevant to installation (I've kept the interface lib), while tools/pybind11Tools.cmake contains all the portion of main CMakeLists.txt devoted to building a module. What can be done from a pybind11 installation:

* ~~import a dependency-bare `pybind11::pybind11` interface lib through pybind11Config, then attach Py headers and c++11/14 compile options, then build lib~~ (this is what this PR used to do)
* import a dependency-rich `pybind11::pybind11` interface lib through pybind11Config. Py headers and c++11/14 are detected and attached at runtime and are sensitive to `PYBIND11_CPP_STANDARD`, etc. Works cmake >= 3.0
* locate pybind11 through pybind11Config, then `include(pybind11Tools)` so that can `pybind11_add_module(name SHARED sources ...)`. Works cmake >= 2.8.12

There are now two additional tests (still bash-driven). `test_installed_target` covers the second bullet above, and `test_installed_module` covers the third bullet point. Note that I had to change the travis install dir b/c I wasn't allowed to install to /usr/local on the 2.8.12 builds. All the Travis tests should now be passing.

The problem is that you're storing a Python `str` in `self.x`, but the `string_view` is pointing to memory owned by a temporary `bytes` containing the UTF-8 encoding (constructed by pybind [here](https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/blob/add56ccdcac23a6c522a2c1174a866e293c61dab/include/pybind11/cast.h#L1139)), which goes away at the end of the call even if you're keeping the `str` alive.

If you had the wrapper class do the UTF-8 encoding and keep a reference to the `bytes`, then passed that to the underlying constructor, it would probably work.

It's indeed a problem with PN53x that an orderly shutdown is required to get them respond next time. With serial connection it's  even more problematic because the baudrate is iinitially ncreased and then the chip  doesnt understand commands if that was not reverted. So key is to catch the relevant signals and properly deinitialize the chip - for Ctrl-C this is done but not for hard kills. For plain KILL it's even questionable because how then to get out of a weird situation. For TERM it might be possible and could be tried with a proper signal handler. But I don't know if the Linux kernel sends TERM before KILL during shutdown.

> Sorry for my delay in replying. Indeed, if mounts or osGroups payload property exist, it is fair to reason that this is a generic worker task payload. I fear that this could be something that changes (e.g. maybe mounts gets added to taskcluster-worker).

We'll have to modify `scriptworker.cot.verify` to support taskcluster-worker once it's ready, so we'll have to make changes then anyway.  I do worry about how we'll detect taskcluster-worker and differentiate it from generic-worker via task definition alone, but we still have the ability to define that.  Worst case, we can curate a set of known workerTypes.

> We don't currently have interactive support, although @grenade is working on this, so at some point soon we will. Machines that are loaned out will be terminated - but I guess an attack avenue might be to stop a machine from being terminated. However, @grenade can advise - I think secrets get wiped as part of the loan process, so it would be impossible for a worker to claim a new task after it has been loaned, I believe, even if the loaner was able to interfere with it and prevent it from being terminated. @grenade can confirm for sure.

Terminating loaners and removing their secrets sounds like a good plan.  In that case, they won't be able to validly sign the cot artifact, and we won't have to worry about otherwise verifying validly-signed tasks aren't run on loaners.

> The gecko windows machines don't have an ssh daemon (although other windows worker types, such as nss worker types do).

Which will be turned off unless it's a loaner, yes?

> A question - if a task doesn't mount anything, and doesn't use osGroups feature - will these checks still work?

It checks to see if `osGroups` is in the task definition.  It can be an empty list.  If generic-worker takes the task even if `osGroups` and `mounts` are not keys in the task definition, then it will cause problems, and we may have to move to the `workerType` solution sooner.  Alternately, we could put a 'workerImpl': 'generic-worker' in the task definition somewhere, and the absence of that or any scriptworker-isms would imply taskcluster-worker.

> Also if there is anything I can do to make it less magic (like adding something in the cot certificate to make it clear it is generic worker) let me know.

We use the worker implementation detection to determine which gpg homedir to use to validate the cot certificate, so it's a bit of a circular dependency.  We could read the cot certificate twice, once ignoring signature to determine worker implementation, second to validate the signature... that may be another option alongside the `workerType` list.

Additional style guide lines:

When a function signature needs spans multiple lines the following formatting must be used:

```python
def function_with_a_lot_of_parameters(
         param1,
         param2,
         ...,
         paramn):
    pass
```

Strings must use single quotes by default, and fallback to double quotes if the contained text contains a single quote (to avoid escaping)

## Video 1: Why SoS Notebook matters
Subtitle: What is multi-language working environment and why does it matter.

1. We are used to working environments for single languages, such as R-Studio for R, Spyder for Python. There are multiple language IDEs (e.g. Eclipse) but each language is separate.

2. Because of the language barriers:
  * There are numerous ways to call another language from one language. E.g. `system` for shell commands, `rpy`, Python matlab engine etc
  * There are numerous attempts to `migrate` good libraries from one language to another (e.g. `ggplot` clone of R in Python)
  * These bridges or clones are immature, difficult to use, but most importantly, not native.

3. SoS Notebook as a multi-language working environment:
  * All languages are local
  * Solves the problem of single environment (for editing, debugging etc)
  * Solves the problem of inter-language data exchange (so not a collection of unrelated scripts)

I've done some research. In python 3, `int` is unlimited [sic] in magnitude on all platforms. numpy has a different set of numeric types which are similar to C. The size of a Tensor is the same on all platforms.

As you've stated, the issue is that the dataset returns a numpy data type (np.int) which has implementation dependent sizes. The size can differ according to machine architecture, OS, C compiler, and other factors. You can't make any assumptions about the size of an np.int. The size could even differ on the same system and same C compiler. Using np.int32 would make for consistent processing across all platforms -- all platforms would raise an error because a LongTensor is expected. LongTensor's are always 64-bits.

There's at least three possible solutions:
1. Have the dataset return np.int64.
2. For places where LongTensor is expected, such as in model.py, force the type to 64-bit using `variable.int64()`. This works for both cpu and gpu types.
3. Change the type earlier in the call sequence. This would help a static type checker. It would be more efficient if the variable undergoes multiple type changes.

http://pytorch-zh.readthedocs.io/en/latest/tensors.html
https://docs.scipy.org/doc/numpy-1.12.0/user/basics.types.html

I think one "root" logger is the way to go. Modules can create sub-loggers by calling `logger.getChild(name)`.

So if you're a top-level Ray file you would do:

```
import ray.services.logger as log
```

Then a module like RLlib could define:

```
import ray.services.logger as root_logger
logger = root_logger.getChild("rllib")
```

And rllib files could do
```
import ray.rllib.logger as log
```

While we still haven't implemented this, now that we have distributed actor handles, it is possible to work around it by using an actor to send messages from the driver to the task. 

Note that this requires the cooperation of the task itself. For example,

```python
import ray
import time

ray.init()

@ray.remote
class TaskCanceler(object):
    def __init__(self):
        self.task_canceled = False

    def cancel_task(self):
        """The driver can call this to cancel the task."""
        self.task_canceled = True

    def is_task_canceled(self):
        """The task calls this to check if it is canceled."""
        return self.task_canceled

@ray.remote
def f(task_canceler):
    i = 0
    while True:
        print(i)
        i += 1
        time.sleep(1)
        # Check if the task should abort.
        if ray.get(task_canceler.is_task_canceled.remote()):
            return

task_canceler = TaskCanceler.remote()

# Create the task.
f.remote(task_canceler)

# Cancel the task.
task_canceler.cancel_task.remote()
```

And of course this can be generalized in a lot of ways (e.g., to use one actor to selectively cancel many different tasks).

- is trio using the asyncio event loop ?
- can it currently be used in a program using already the asyncio event loop ?
- if no will it allow it in the future ?

I don't particularly like fiddling with getpid() all the time, that's 99.999999% nonproductive work trying to catch the one mistake somebody made. I can't think of a way to get a worse usefulness/required_work ratio than that.

IMHO the best solution would be to `os.register_at_fork` an `after_in_child` handler which monkeypatch-poisons the current Trio mainloop and/or task so that it'll fall flat on its face the next time it's entered, preferably in such a way that there's a semi-understandable error message. That at least would cause no additional busywork for the common case (no-fork-ever).

There's a lot of important new public API here, and I want to take some time to think it over. Some particular concerns are that it doesn't work on Windows (appveyor is failing due to missing `fcntl`, and more fundamentally there's the problem that most ways of connecting to a child stdin/out/err on Windows don't support non-blocking operation), and I'm not at all convinced `wait_for_child` and `{Read,Write}FdStream` should be public APIs at all. (As opposed to telling people to call `Process.wait`, and that `Process.std{in,out,err}` are streams of some unspecified concrete type.)

I know you want to get some kind of subprocess support into trio-asyncio ASAP. To check I'm understanding right: for the way you're doing this in trio-asyncio currently, you're delegating most of the work to asyncio's existing support for Unix subprocesses, and the two integration points with Trio are (a) the new `wait_for_child` call, (b) you assume that Trio's `wait_{read,write}able` functions work on subprocess pipes. Is that correct?

In the long run I suspect that we'll want to rework how subprocesses are handled in trio-asyncio so that we have our own implementation of `asyncio.SubprocessTransport` that directly wraps `trio.Process` (or whatever we end up calling it). The reason I think this is that there are a *lot* of nasty details on getting subprocesses working reliably cross-platform, and asyncio doesn't do a particularly good job here (in particular, their `SelectorEventLoop` doesn't support subprocesses at all on Windows). And these are problems we're going to eventually need to solve in trio anyway. So for trio-asyncio, instead of mostly using asyncio's subprocess code and forwarding a few bits and pieces to trio, I think in the end we'll be better off replacing the whole thing.

But this is also part of why I'm reluctant to add `wait_for_child` to Trio itself – the one use case seems to be temporary scaffolding to get something working now, but that won't be part of a long-term solution.

Maybe it would make sense to move the `wait_for_child` code into trio-asyncio for now, if you're in a hurry to get that working?

(And BTW, `wait_for_child`/`ProcessWaiter` look like they could be substantially simplified if they're only intended to handle this one case. There seems to be some stray code left over from previous versions, and I don't know why there's Windows-specific code in there, given that (a) this PR doesn't support Windows, (b) `os.waitpid` works on Windows anyway, or at least is documented to? I could be missing things though, I didn't look very carefully.)

> But in any case, ok, how would you implement something like functools.partial in Python?

Instead of looking at the last frame you can traverse until you see `f_inawait`. Isn't ideal though.

TBH I didn't think that you want both things at the same time: 
* better reporting about forgotten await (prohibit calling coroutines without await);
*  **and** a way to call coroutines without await (for partials).  

I thought you want something as radical as PEP 3152, so my PoC does exactly that: it tries to enforce instantiating coroutines only in await expressions. `functools.partial` works by an accident.

> What do you mean by "wouldn't handle"?

I mean that `__acall__` will be used only by the `await` expression. The idea was to throw an error when a user simply calls a coroutine but doesn't await on it. **But** if you want to support partials (and code that creates similar wrappers) both our proposals wouldn't work. 

You can only pick one thing: 
* always raise an error if a coroutine is called without an await.
* allow coroutines to be called, to support wrappers/partials/decorators/etc (status quo).

Longer term, I wonder if we should relax the type-checking in `wait_socket_*` to make them accept file descriptors (or technically `SOCKET` handles on windows), or any object with a `fileno` method. It looks like there are a few reasons why we currently restrict to just `socket` objects:

- `wait_socket_*` accepted random file descriptors on Unix, then it would be possible to write code that accidentally passes non-socket file descriptors into `wait_socket_*`, and it would seem to work, but then when you tried running your program on Windows it would break. Forcing the use of socket objects makes it harder to make this mistake. This seems like a relatively minor benefit though – the correct way to write such code is to use the plain `wait_readable`/`wait_writable` and that... also gives you a program that works on Unix but not Windows.

- Using socket objects in the Windows backend gives us a bit of robustness against some weird edge cases involving sockets getting closed while we're in the middle of selecting on them. But these are pretty rare cases, and #36 is a much more general solution that we need to implement anyway, so this doesn't seem like a big deal either.

- Maybe `select.select` on Windows only handles `socket` objects, not socket descriptors? Not that fixing this would be a big deal, it'd just mean making our own `select` wrapper which we should possibly do at some point anyway (#3). ...OK, no, but I just checked and it's not a problem anyway, `select.select` is happy to take raw socket descriptors:

```
In [8]: select.select([s2], [s2], [s2])
Out[8]:
([],
 [<socket.socket fd=684, family=AddressFamily.AF_INET, type=SocketKind.SOCK_STREAM, proto=0, laddr=('127.0.0.1', 49819), raddr=('127.0.0.1', 49814)>],
 [])

In [9]: select.select([s2.fileno()], [s2.fileno()], [s2.fileno()])
Out[9]: ([], [684], [])
```

So I guess we should probably start allowing socket descriptors in `wait_socket_*` (maybe after first fixing #3). Then you could just do `await wait_socket_readable(pgconn)` directly. Thanks for bringing this up!

Hi Johannes,

to correctly obtain Sentinel-2 L2A data using `WmsRequest` or `WcsRequest` you basically have to do 2 things:

- Set parameter `layer` to a layer which is defined in Sentinel Hub Configurator to use Sentinel-2 L2A source. (You did this correctly.)
- Set parameter `data_source` to `DataSource.SENTINEL2_L2A`. (This is what you are missing.)

It is a bit redundant to specify the same thing in 2 different places but at the moment but the services and the Python package have to get this info separately. For the future we have plans to improve that.

More examples about data source specification in [documentation](http://sentinelhub-py.readthedocs.io/en/latest/examples/ogc_request.html#Data-Sources). (I will try to make it a bit clearer for this part.)

This is currently in the master branch with better text in the devel branch.  The question of performance depends on how often that an exception is issues.  The default case in python is that exceptions should be fairly light as it is supposed to be okay to try first and ask permission later.  However, this is a runtime  exception in which there is likely not going to be a good recovery, thus giving good feed back should not be an issue.  If we are concerned about performance, then we for this we should cache the alternative methods string on the first occurrence.

We will have a similar problem when we consider tracing information.  For the ease of users it is a good idea to have a fully integrated tracing output between Java, C++  and Python so that all the excecution path is clear.  But this means more overhead as we have to fake a traceback on the exception handling to create a object that knows the stack dump with the Java exception, the C++ stack and the existing python.  Python has pretty low overhead as it just keeps track of the internal execution stack.  But JPype does not have that option.  Thus we are going to need to decide which exception paths need to follow the Python philosophy and run with low overhead (say array out of bounds) and which we should pay the full price of clarity.
